text: The laparoscopic view reveals the left pelvic sidewall at the level of the psoas muscle, where the surgeon begins surgical dissection by releasing the congenital attachment of the sigmoid colon.

The surgeon uses an ultrasonic scalpel, known as the harmonic device, to dissect and release advanced congenital adhesions of the sigmoid colon, which extend over adnexal structures and may reach the anterior abdominal wall. This manipulation aims to transform the sigmoid colon into a more retractile structure.

The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to drop down the congenital adhesions of the sigmoid colon, revealing the left adnexa. The psoas muscle becomes visible above the harmonic scalpel, indicating the need for adequate exposure before continuing the dissection.
The surgeon uses small movements of the harmonic scalpel to progressively increase exposure, revealing the psoas muscle above the bowel holding grasper. The muscle is fully exposed in this patient.

The surgeon opens the round ligament as laterally as possible, identifying the junction with the obliterated umbilical artery. The round ligament is divided at this level, revealing the obliterated umbilical vessel behind the divided end, which serves as a landmark for navigating the lateral side wall.

The surgeon excises the peritoneum parallel to the infundibulopelvic ligament, exposing the psoas muscle and its tendon. The external iliac artery is visible on the left side of the screen. The incision opens up the pelvic spaces, revealing the ureter to the right of the harmonic device, the obliterated umbilical artery to the left, and the uterine artery crossing over the ureter.

The surgeon holds the obliterated umbilical artery and tugs on the superior vesical artery, causing a visible movement of the bladder. The medial paravesical space is developed medial to the obliterated umbilical artery, followed by the lateral paravesical fossa. The dissection progresses deep enough to reach the levator floor and the ileococcygeus muscle group.

The surgeon uses both hands to perform small dissection movements, reaching the depth of the lateral pelvis. The broad ligament is opened, with the infundibulopelvic ligament held in the left hand and the assistant's grasper showing the ureter. The infundibulopelvic ligament is coagulated and divided, creating wide exposure. The posterior leaf of the broad ligament is opened, and the incision extends into the pararectal fossa, developing the medial pararectal space medial to the ureter. The harmonic device is used like a pencil to demarcate the line, merging the pararectal space with the rectovaginal space centrally.

The surgeon identifies the hypogastric nerve using Babcock's forceps, carefully delineating it with the harmonic device. The nerve is preferentially applied towards the mesorectum, positioned approximately two centimeters deep from the ureter level, and is lateralized for preservation.

The surgeon develops the iliac tree and lateral pararectal fossa, revealing the uterine artery coursing medially upwards. The deep uterine vein is visible below the uterine artery. The ureter is seen traveling between the uterine artery and vein, and is dissected using the harmonic blade. The surgeon holds the uterine artery with the left hand and divides its branch to the ureter, facilitating easier dissection of the ureter.

The surgeon holds the ureter with the left hand and begins deperitonizing it, removing its attachment to the posterior leaf of the broad ligament. The assistant uses a grasper to retrovert the uterus and push down the bladder, exposing the medial pillar or anterior parametra, which is held and dissected. The bladder is dissected to develop the uterovesical and vesicovaginal spaces, crucial for completing the ureteric dissection to the bladder.

The surgeon dissects the medial pillar, also known as the anterior parametra, to unroof the ureter. The uterine artery, preserved until this point, is coagulated and divided. As the dissection deepens, the vaginal artery is identified traveling towards the vagina. The deep uterine vein, possibly the vaginal vein, is noted to travel below the uterine artery and ureter, with nerves positioned deep to this vein.

The surgeon opens the pelvic triangle window on the right side, revealing the external iliac vein as a blue structure. As the incision is made upwards, the obliterated umbilical vessel becomes visible, serving as an important landmark for delineating the iliac tree.

As the surgical space expands, the surgeon identifies branches of the iliac tree, including the uterine artery and the superior vesicle artery. The superior vesicle artery is manipulated, showing movement towards the bladder. The uterine artery is visible traversing horizontally. Deeper dissection reveals the vaginal vessels and the inferior vesicle artery near the ischial spine. The ureter is observed on the left side, attached to the peritoneal leaf.

The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the ureter traveling beneath the uterine artery. The deep uterine vein is visible draining into the internal iliac vein. The vaginal artery, identified as a white structure, is seen traveling towards the paracord pores. The assistant uses a grasper to lift the uterine artery, allowing dissection on the superior surface of the ureter to lateralize it further, tracing its path to the bladder.

The surgeon performs diuretic dissection, revealing the deep uterine vein located beneath the ureter. The ureter is held with a Babcock clamp and then released, while the uterine artery remains undivided and tented above the ureter. The vesicle vein is visible draining into the deep uterine vein. Using a harmonic device, the surgeon tugs on the vertical blue vein draining into the deep uterine vein. The uterine artery is skeletonized from its origin to the internal os, showing its upward turn as the ascending uterine artery. The medial pillar is divided, completing the tunnel dissection, and the ureter rolls into the bladder with the uterine artery fully separated.

The surgeon identifies the uterine artery and hypogastric nerve, along with the vaginal vessels, which include the deep uterine vein and vaginal artery. The lateral parametra, consisting of the uterine artery and its fascia, is dissected towards the uterine side, enhancing the visibility of the anterior parametra. The uterine artery is noted for its toughness, elasticity, coiled structure, and length.

The assistant grasper holds the posterior utero-sacral ligament as the surgeon completes the hysterectomy. The harmonic device cuts into the peritoneum, developing the rectovaginal space. The surgeon exposes the two layers of the Denonvillous fascia and delineates the utero-sacral ligament, also known as the sacro-uterine ligament, which forms the posterior parametra. The rectal serosa is visible, and the surgeon identifies the facial structures attaching the rectum to the vagina, known as the rectovaginal ligament.
